caption: Results of the in limbo approach on actual fMRI-data from Forstmann et al. [19], using different z-thresholds of 2.3, 3.1 and 3.7, corresponding to uncorrected single-sided α-values of p <0.01, p <0.001 and p <0.0001.After this initial thresholding procedure, these surviving areas were corrected at the cluster-level using Gaussian random field theory. The in limbo areas are not significantly different from the least-significantly activated brain area, according to a t-test with an alpha-level of p<0.05, uncorrected for multiple comparisons. Legend: Yellow-red areas are significantly activated, green areas are in limbo. Note how with a rather liberal threshold of 2.3 almost the entire brain is in limbo and increasing the z-threshold from 3.1 to 3.7 does not change much in volume of brain that is in limbo. Brains are in MNI-152 space with slices at X = 42, Y = 65, Z = 65.

A2: This image depicts the brain.
A3: The caption does not specify any abnormalities identified in this image. It mostly discusses threshold variations and their impact on image interpretation.
A4: The image shows various levels of brain activation, not necessarily irregularities or abnormalities. But final interpretation depends on the purpose of the analysis.

A5: Yes, the image contains labels or indexes that are significant. The z-threshold values of 2.3, 3.1, and 3.7, corresponding to uncorrected single-sided α-values of p <0.01, p <0.001 and p <0.0001, are noteworthy. Furthermore, the labels for significantly activated areas (yellow-red) and "in limbo" areas (green) are quite significant. The brain's coordinates in MNI-152 space (X = 42, Y = 65, Z = 65) are also noteworthy.
